h1Title: Fibromuscular Dysplasia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) is a non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic arterial disease that primarily affects medium-sized arteries in young-to-middle-aged women, causing abnormal fibrous and muscular overgrowth within the vessel wall. The renal arteries (in up to 60-75% of cases) and carotid and vertebral arteries (in up to 75%) are most commonly affected, leading to renovascular hypertension, stroke, and arterial dissection. Prevalence in the general population is estimated at 3-4 per 1,000, though asymptomatic disease is likely underdiagnosed. FMD is not caused by plaque buildup and does not respond to cholesterol-lowering therapy; percutaneous transluminal angioplasty achieves sustained blood pressure normalization or reduction in 40-60% of renal FMD patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 3-4 per 1,000 adults; symptomatic disease diagnosed predominantly in women, accounting for 85-90% of registry cases (US FMD Registry, 2012)
keyStats.demographics: 85-90% female; median age at diagnosis 52 years (US FMD Registry); multifocal renal involvement in 35-40% of diagnosed patients
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 52 years, but disease process begins in younger adult life; some cases diagnosed in adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 1-2 million individuals affected in the US alone; true global prevalence unknown due to underdiagnosis

definition: Fibromuscular dysplasia (ICD-10: I77.3) is a systemic non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic arteriopathy characterized by abnormal cellular proliferation, fibrosis, and architectural disorganization in the medial and intimal layers of medium-caliber arteries. The disease is classified by histological layer and morphological pattern. Multifocal FMD — the classic 'string of beads' angiographic appearance caused by alternating areas of arterial narrowing and dilation in the medial layer — accounts for more than 80% of cases and is almost exclusively seen in women. Focal FMD produces a single discrete concentric or tubular narrowing without the beaded pattern and can occur in men and younger patients. Affected arteries in order of frequency are: renal (60-75%), internal carotid and vertebral (up to 75% in series using cerebrovascular imaging), mesenteric, hepatic, and iliac. Bilateral involvement is common, particularly in renal and carotid beds. The cause of the fibromuscular proliferation is not fully understood; genetic predisposition, hemodynamic stress (mechanical stretch from turbulent flow), and hormonal influences are all implicated. Cardiology, vascular surgery, nephrology, and neurology all participate in management depending on the affected territory. Unlike atherosclerosis, FMD is not associated with traditional cardiovascular risk factors such as dyslipidemia or diabetes.

typesClassification.description: The most common subtype (>80%). Creates the characteristic 'string of beads' angiographic appearance — alternating segments of stenosis and dilation — in the mid-to-distal renal or carotid arteries. Almost exclusively affects women in the fourth to sixth decades. Risk of arterial dissection and aneurysm is present but lower than focal FMD.

Produces a single smooth tubular narrowing without beading, typically at the proximal artery. Histologically shows medial or intimal fibroplasia. Occurs in men and younger patients more often than multifocal disease. Associated with a higher risk of spontaneous arterial dissection and may require endovascular treatment more urgently.

A rare subtype with collagen replacing the outer medial layer, producing severe stenoses without dilation. Predominantly affects the renal artery in women. Uncommon and requires expert histological confirmation.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset severe hypertension in a young woman (age under 50) without a family history of essential hypertension or kidney disease — the classic presentation of renal artery FMD causing renovascular hypertension.
Refractory hypertension poorly controlled on two or more antihypertensive agents — renovascular hypertension from FMD should be excluded, particularly in premenopausal women.

Pulsatile tinnitus — a whooshing or swooshing sound synchronous with the heartbeat, caused by turbulent flow through a fibrodysplastic carotid artery; reported in up to 70% of patients with cervical FMD.
Unilateral or bilateral neck pain without trauma, associated with pulsatile headache, in the context of cervicocephalic FMD.
Transient ischemic attack (TIA) or ischemic stroke in a young adult without atherosclerotic risk factors — carotid or vertebral FMD with dissection or thrombus embolism.
Pulsatile headache or migraine-like episodes, reported in 50-60% of patients with cervicocephalic involvement, possibly from baroreceptor dysfunction in a dysplastic carotid artery.
Flank pain or abdominal bruit over the renal artery in a young hypertensive patient — the abdominal bruit is audible in approximately 40% of renal FMD cases on careful auscultation.
Acute severe abdominal or back pain from spontaneous mesenteric or renal artery dissection, a recognized acute complication of FMD.
Facial droop, arm weakness, or speech disturbance from vertebral artery dissection with posterior circulation ischemia.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset hypertension in a woman under 50 without obesity, family history of essential hypertension, or metabolic risk factors — this demographic should prompt renal artery imaging.
Persistent unexplained pulsatile tinnitus in a woman, particularly if unilateral and without otological explanation.
Mild unilateral neck pain or tenderness following a minor Valsalva maneuver (coughing, vomiting, straining) — potentially representing early carotid FMD or early dissection.
A faint abdominal bruit on physical examination in a young hypertensive patient, audible between the umbilicus and costal margin.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe thunderclap headache, altered consciousness, or focal neurological deficit — may signal carotid or vertebral artery dissection causing stroke; requires immediate CT angiography of the neck and brain.

Acute-onset hypertension with neurological symptoms, visual changes, or pulmonary edema suggesting a hypertensive emergency from bilateral renal artery involvement or contralateral compensatory dysfunction.

Sudden severe flank or periumbilical pain with hemodynamic instability — spontaneous renal or mesenteric artery dissection or rupture of a FMD-related aneurysm demands emergency vascular imaging and surgery.
Acute paraplegia or severe limb ischemia from spinal cord or peripheral arterial involvement in the rare spinal or iliac variants of FMD.

causes.cause: Genetic predisposition (autosomal dominant with variable penetrance)
causes.description: Familial clustering is reported in 7-11% of cases. Genome-wide association studies have identified associations with PHACTR1, a regulator of actin cytoskeleton and endothelial function. No single dominant mutation has been identified; FMD is likely polygenic, with multiple variants contributing cumulatively to arterial wall fragility.

FMD predominantly affects mid-to-distal segments of vessels subject to repeated flexion, tethering, and pulsatile stretch — the renal and carotid arteries are tethered at specific anatomical points. Repetitive mechanical trauma is thought to trigger abnormal fibromuscular repair responses, explaining the predilection for specific segments over the vessel's entire length.

The strong female predominance (85-90%) and the peak incidence in reproductive-age and perimenopausal women implicate sex hormones in pathogenesis. Estrogen receptors are expressed on arterial smooth muscle and may modulate the fibroblast and smooth muscle proliferative response to mechanical injury. The precise hormonal mechanism is not yet defined.

Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor identified in FMD registries, with smokers representing 30-40% of patients in the US FMD Registry. Nicotine causes direct vascular smooth muscle and endothelial injury and may amplify the abnormal proliferative response in genetically susceptible individuals.

A subset of FMD patients have features overlapping with connective tissue disorders — joint hypermobility, skin extensibility, or spontaneous arterial dissection at multiple sites. This suggests shared pathobiology involving extracellular matrix components such as fibronectin and type III collagen, though FMD does not fulfill diagnostic criteria for Ehlers-Danlos or Marfan syndromes in most cases.

riskFactors.description: Women account for 85-90% of all FMD cases across international registries. The sex disparity is most pronounced for multifocal FMD. Men represent a minority but are over-represented in focal, dissection-prone subtypes.

Symptomatic presentation peaks in the fourth to sixth decades, though the underlying arterial changes likely begin in young adult life. Adolescent and pediatric cases are reported, predominantly in the renal territory.
Active smoking is present in 30-40% of FMD patients, roughly twice the population prevalence. Cessation is strongly recommended but evidence that cessation alters disease progression is indirect.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of FMD or arterial dissection
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of FMD patients have an estimated 7-11% risk of having FMD themselves. A family history of unexplained stroke, aneurysm, or arterial dissection in a young person should prompt screening.
Joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, or a personal or family history of spontaneous arterial dissection are associated with FMD and suggest a shared matrix-vulnerability phenotype.

Observed epidemiologically in some registry series; the proposed mechanism is repetitive hormonal and hemodynamic stress on the renal vasculature during multiple pregnancies, but this association is not firmly established.

A variant in the PHACTR1 locus (rs9349379-A) identified by GWAS is associated with FMD, cervical artery dissection, and migraine. This allele is present in approximately 35-40% of FMD patients vs 25% of controls in the largest cohort study (Debette et al., Nature Genetics 2015).

affectedDemographics: Women of European descent aged 40-60 years represent the largest identified group; the US FMD Registry is over 90% female and over 90% white.
Younger women aged 20-40 with refractory hypertension are disproportionately represented in the renal FMD subgroup, since essential hypertension is uncommon at these ages.
Men with FMD are more likely to present with dissection or intracranial aneurysm and less likely to have the classic multifocal beaded pattern.
Patients with concurrent diagnoses of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome hypermobile type or PHACTR1-related arteriopathies represent an overlapping genetic subgroup.
diagnosisOverview: FMD is diagnosed by arterial imaging — the key is identifying the characteristic morphology in one or more arterial beds. Duplex ultrasound of renal and carotid arteries is the first-line investigation: in renal FMD, peak systolic velocity above 200 cm/s at the mid-to-distal artery, or a distinct flow turbulence pattern, suggests stenosis. CT angiography (CTA) and MR angiography (MRA) provide more complete anatomical detail and are preferred for confirming the diagnosis and assessing multivessel involvement. CTA has the advantage of excellent spatial resolution and is preferred when detecting small aneurysms or arterial dissection; MRA avoids ionizing radiation and is preferred for follow-up imaging. Conventional digital subtraction angiography (DSA) remains the gold standard for characterizing the 'string-of-beads' pattern definitively and for simultaneous percutaneous transluminal angioplasty if stenosis is confirmed — DSA should be reserved for cases where endovascular treatment is planned or non-invasive imaging is inconclusive. All patients diagnosed with FMD in one territory should undergo imaging of other major vascular beds — at minimum, the renal and cervical arteries, plus intracranial imaging to detect aneurysms. Plasma renin activity (PRA) and aldosterone levels support the diagnosis of functional renovascular hypertension but are neither specific nor sensitive enough to confirm or exclude FMD alone. The 2019 AHA Scientific Statement on FMD recommends complete vascular imaging at diagnosis to exclude multifocal disease.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography (CTA) of the renal and cervical arteries
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line confirmatory imaging; identifies the string-of-beads morphology, arterial dissection, and associated aneurysms in the renal, carotid, and vertebral arteries
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute CT scan with intravenous contrast injection via an arm vein. The patient lies still while the scanner captures detailed arterial images. Mild warmth is felt during contrast injection. Results usually reviewed by radiology within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Duplex ultrasound of renal and carotid arteries
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive first-line screen; detects elevated flow velocity through FMD stenoses and can visualize the mid-renal or cervical carotid artery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel-covered ultrasound probe is moved over the flank (renal) or neck (carotid) for 20-40 minutes. Completely painless. Results are available on the same day. Lower sensitivity than CTA for mid-vessel lesions in obese patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Preferred radiation-free imaging for surveillance; characterizes vessel morphology without contrast in many protocols; detects dissection, aneurysm, and multi-territory involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner. No radiation exposure. Gadolinium contrast may be used. Claustrophobia can limit tolerability; open-bore MRI is an alternative. Better for follow-up than for first-time evaluation of subtle lesions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test; provides highest resolution images of the beaded pattern; performed when angioplasty is planned at the same session
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed via a catheter inserted through the groin or wrist under local anaesthesia and sedation. Contrast is injected directly into the artery. A 1-3 hour procedure; patient is observed for 4-6 hours afterward for access-site complications. Definitive diagnosis and treatment in one procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports functional significance of renal artery FMD; elevated PRA in the appropriate clinical context indicates renin-mediated hypertension from reduced renal perfusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw taken supine or upright after standardized conditions (sodium intake, medications held if possible). Results in 2-5 days depending on laboratory. Confirms renovascular hypertension but does not diagnose FMD directly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies blood pressure burden, detects masked hypertension, and provides baseline data before and after percutaneous angioplasty to assess procedural response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small BP cuff worn on the non-dominant arm for 24 hours, taking readings every 15-30 minutes. Mildly disruptive to sleep but not painful. Readings are downloaded at clinic review the following day.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy in FMD depends on the affected territory, symptom burden, and disease morphology. The 2019 AHA Scientific Statement on FMD and the 2019 European consensus document provide the current evidence framework. For renovascular FMD causing hypertension, percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) without stenting is the preferred revascularization strategy for both multifocal and focal subtypes — balloon dilation alone typically restores luminal caliber effectively, and routine stenting is discouraged because FMD is not plaque-based and stents in dysplastic arteries carry higher long-term complication rates. PTA achieves blood pressure cure (normalization without medication) in 20-40% and improvement in 40-60% of renal FMD patients, with best results in young women with short disease duration. For cervicocephalic FMD causing TIA or dissection, antiplatelet therapy — aspirin 75-100 mg daily or clopidogrel — is first-line; anticoagulation with heparin followed by warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant for 3-6 months is preferred when arterial dissection or intraluminal thrombus is identified. Cervical PTA with or without stenting is reserved for cases with recurrent neurological events despite antiplatelet therapy. Hypertension management with ACE inhibitors or ARBs and calcium channel blockers is effective for blood pressure control when revascularization is not indicated or is incomplete. Pulsatile tinnitus from carotid FMD usually improves after successful PTA. All patients should receive antiplatelet therapy and be enrolled in a multidisciplinary FMD clinic for long-term surveillance with periodic imaging every 3-5 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for all patients with cervicocephalic FMD and for renal FMD patients not undergoing immediate revascularization. Reduces thromboembolic risk from turbulent flow, arterial dissection flap, and small ulcerative lesions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stroke recurrence by approximately 22% in arterial dissection and TIA — extrapolated from dissection and TIA trials; FMD-specific RCT data are lacking

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg daily; losartan 50-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone of blood pressure management in renovascular hypertension when RAAS activation is the mechanism. Particularly effective in renin-mediated FMD hypertension. Bilateral renal FMD requires careful monitoring of creatinine and potassium after initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Blood pressure reduction of 10-15 mmHg systolic in renin-mediated renovascular hypertension; renal function monitoring mandatory in bilateral or severe unilateral disease

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium channel blockers (e.g., amlodipine 5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Added for persistent hypertension after RAAS inhibition, or used as monotherapy when ACE inhibitors are contraindicated. No direct effect on the arterial dysplasia but effective for blood pressure control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-15 mmHg as monotherapy; effective adjunct in combination antihypertensive regimens

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulation (heparin then DOAC or warfarin for 3-6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated when FMD presents with or is complicated by arterial dissection and intraluminal thrombus. Prevents propagation of the dissecting haematoma and embolic occlusion of distal branches.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard-of-care for spontaneous arterial dissection; dissection recanalization occurs in 80-90% of patients treated with either antiplatelets or anticoagulation at 6 months (CADISS trial)

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) — renal or cervical
surgicalOptions.description: Catheter-based balloon dilation of the stenotic segment performed via femoral or radial artery access. For renal FMD, wire and balloon are navigated under fluoroscopy to the mid-to-distal renal artery; the balloon is inflated to fracture the fibromuscular rings and restore luminal diameter. Stenting is avoided unless PTA fails.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success >95%; blood pressure cure (normalization without medication) in 20-40%; blood pressure improvement in 40-60%; durable in 80-90% at 5 years (US FMD Registry outcomes)

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid or vertebral artery stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for cervicocephalic FMD with recurrent TIA or stroke on antiplatelet therapy, or failed balloon PTA. Stent placed to maintain luminal patency across the dysplastic segment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success >90%; recurrent neurological events after stenting reduced to approximately 5-8% per year in observational series

surgicalOptions.name: Open surgical renal artery revascularization
surgicalOptions.description: Aortorenal bypass or ex-vivo repair reserved for complex anatomical disease, failed PTA, long-segment FMD, or coexisting renal artery aneurysm requiring reconstruction. Rarely required in contemporary practice.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Blood pressure cure or improvement in 80-90% of selected cases; 5-year patency above 90% in experienced surgical centers

whySeeSpecialist: FMD requires a multidisciplinary approach. Vascular medicine, interventional radiology or vascular surgery, and hypertension nephrology should all be involved — the choice of antiplatelet vs revascularization and the imaging protocol for multiterritory surveillance need expert coordination. For cerebrovascular FMD with TIA or dissection, urgent neurology assessment is required.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a clinician at a center with a dedicated FMD multidisciplinary team, ideally a center affiliated with the US FMD Registry or the European FMD Initiative. Interventional radiologists or vascular surgeons with experience in renal and cervical PTA without routine stenting produce the best technical outcomes. Hypertension specialists with expertise in renovascular hypertension optimize medical management.
hospitalCriteria: CTA and MRA capability for multiterritory imaging
Interventional radiology service with renal and cervical PTA experience
Dedicated vascular medicine or multidisciplinary FMD clinic
Neurovascular unit for carotid and vertebral dissection management
keyFacilities: Academic vascular medicine centers with FMD programs
Neurovascular intensive care units for acute cervical artery dissection
preventionStrategies: Eliminate tobacco smoking — the single most impactful modifiable risk factor identified in FMD registries.
Screen first-degree relatives of FMD patients with duplex ultrasound of renal and carotid arteries if they develop unexplained hypertension or neurological symptoms.
Report new pulsatile tinnitus, neck pain, or sudden headache to a clinician promptly, as early detection prevents stroke from arterial dissection.

Avoid extreme mechanical neck manipulation (high-velocity chiropractic adjustment) in patients with known or suspected cervicocephalic FMD, as manipulation has been associated with cervical artery dissection.
Maintain optimal blood pressure through regular monitoring and adherence to antihypertensive therapy pending revascularization.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking completely — tobacco exposure likely accelerates fibromuscular proliferative activity and the risk of dissection.
Monitor blood pressure at home daily with a calibrated device; maintain logs for clinic review.
Follow up with imaging surveillance every 3-5 years as recommended — new arterial beds can become symptomatic, and existing lesions can progress.
Report any new neurological symptoms, sudden headache, or neck pain immediately, as these can signal arterial dissection requiring urgent imaging.
Inform all healthcare providers, dentists, and practitioners before any procedure involving neck manipulation or invasive arterial work.

recommended: Potassium-rich foods (avocado, spinach, bananas, potatoes) to support blood pressure control on RAAS-inhibitor therapy
Low-sodium diet (<2 g/day) to optimize antihypertensive response
Mediterranean diet pattern — associated with favorable vascular outcomes in general population data
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L water daily) to maintain renal perfusion
avoid: Excess sodium from processed foods, which worsens renin-mediated hypertension
High-dose NSAIDs in patients on antiplatelet therapy, which increase bleeding risk
Stimulants (high-dose caffeine, sympathomimetics) that raise blood pressure acutely

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — 30 minutes of brisk walking or cycling 5 days per week — is safe and recommended for blood pressure management once FMD is diagnosed and blood pressure is controlled. Contact sports and activities associated with high-force neck traction should be avoided in cervicocephalic FMD. Patients with intracranial aneurysms on surveillance should avoid activities that cause sudden blood pressure spikes (heavy isometric lifting) pending neurosurgery assessment.
dailyManagement: Take antihypertensive medications consistently each day; missed doses allow blood pressure to rise, increasing shear stress on dysplastic arterial segments.
Record home blood pressure twice daily (morning and evening) and bring the log to clinic; target blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg.
Take aspirin with food; report unusual bruising or melena to your clinician.
Contact emergency services immediately for sudden severe headache, neck pain, visual loss, or one-sided weakness — do not wait for a routine appointment.
Carry a medical alert card or app note indicating FMD and antiplatelet use, relevant for emergency care providers.
prognosis: Prognosis in FMD is generally favorable with appropriate management. Renovascular FMD treated with PTA normalizes or substantially improves blood pressure in 60-80% of patients, reducing the long-term risk of hypertensive end-organ damage. Cervicocephalic FMD managed with antiplatelet therapy carries a low annual stroke recurrence rate of roughly 2-3% after the initial event. Dissection-related stroke outcomes depend on the extent of ischemia at presentation — most minor strokes recover well with anticoagulation and rehabilitation. FMD does not typically cause progressive arterial failure analogous to atherosclerosis, but approximately 30-40% of patients have new lesions or progression on follow-up imaging at 5 years. Intracranial aneurysms, present in 7-12% of FMD patients, carry a small annual rupture risk of 0.5-1% that requires lifelong surveillance. Overall, patients managed in dedicated FMD programs show quality-of-life equivalent to age-matched controls.
recoveryTimeline: Blood pressure response to renal PTA is typically evident within 1-4 weeks; full blood pressure benefit is assessed at 3 months. Neurological recovery from dissection-related TIA or minor stroke occurs over 1-6 months with antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy. Pulsatile tinnitus from carotid FMD improves within days to weeks after successful PTA in most patients.
complications: Spontaneous arterial dissection — the most serious acute complication, occurring in up to 25% of cervicocephalic FMD patients; presents as sudden neck pain, headache, and focal neurological deficit; requires urgent anticoagulation or PTA.
Ischemic stroke or TIA from carotid or vertebral artery dissection or thrombus embolism — screen with CT or MRI brain immediately when neurological symptoms develop.
Intracranial aneurysm — present in 7-12% of FMD patients; monitor with annual or biannual MRA; aneurysms above 7 mm require neurosurgical or endovascular assessment.
Renal artery aneurysm — occurs in 5-15% of renal FMD cases; small aneurysms are observed; those above 2 cm or enlarging require endovascular or surgical repair.

Hypertensive end-organ damage — chronic uncontrolled renovascular hypertension from untreated FMD causes left ventricular hypertrophy, renal impairment, and stroke; monitor with echocardiography and serum creatinine.
Restenosis after PTA — occurs in 10-20% of treated renal arteries within 5 years; detected on surveillance duplex imaging; repeat PTA usually successful.

supportResources.name: US FMD Registry — FMD Society of America
supportResources.description: Patient registry, education, and community support for fibromuscular dysplasia patients in North America.

supportResources.name: European FMD Initiative (ARCADIA Registry)
supportResources.description: European multicenter registry and research network providing clinical guidance and patient resources for FMD.

supportResources.name: AHA Scientific Statement on FMD
supportResources.description: 2019 American Heart Association comprehensive clinical guidance document on the diagnosis and management of FMD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aortic Dissection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aortic dissection involves the large aorta and presents with tearing chest or back pain, hemodynamic instability, and mediastinal widening on imaging. FMD affects medium-sized arteries (renal, carotid) and presents as renovascular hypertension or cervical dissection. Aortic dissection is a surgical emergency; FMD is managed medically or with PTA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral Artery Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral artery disease is atherosclerotic, affects older patients with dyslipidemia and diabetes, and causes claudication from fixed plaque stenosis of limb arteries. FMD is non-atherosclerotic, affects young women, and creates fibromuscullar beaded stenoses in visceral and cervical arteries without dyslipidemia. Treatment differs fundamentally — statins do not help FMD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Essential Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Essential hypertension is multifactorial, common, and affects older adults with polygenic predisposition plus metabolic risk factors. Renovascular FMD causes secondary hypertension through renin-mediated mechanisms in young women without typical risk factors. FMD is distinguished by elevated plasma renin activity, abdominal bruit, and the characteristic arterial morphology on imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Aortic Dissection
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the mechanism of non-atherosclerotic arterial wall weakness; FMD patients have elevated rates of concurrent spontaneous arterial dissection in multiple territories.

relatedConditions.name: Renal Artery Stenosis
relatedConditions.relevance: FMD is the second most common cause of renal artery stenosis after atherosclerosis; both cause renovascular hypertension but through entirely different mechanisms.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Carotid and vertebral FMD is a leading cause of stroke in young adults via dissection or embolic mechanisms.

faqs.question: What is fibromuscular dysplasia?
faqs.answer: Fibromuscular dysplasia is a non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic disease of the arterial wall, causing abnormal fibrous and muscular overgrowth in medium-sized arteries. It predominantly affects the renal and carotid arteries in young-to-middle-aged women, leading to renovascular hypertension, arterial dissection, and stroke risk.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of fibromuscular dysplasia?
faqs.answer: The most common symptoms are difficult-to-control hypertension in a young woman, pulsatile tinnitus (a whooshing sound in the ear), neck pain, and headaches. Stroke or TIA can occur from carotid or vertebral artery involvement. Renal artery FMD may be asymptomatic and detected only on imaging for hypertension workup.

faqs.question: Who gets fibromuscular dysplasia?
faqs.answer: FMD affects predominantly women, who account for 85-90% of cases. The median age at diagnosis is 52 years, though the disease starts earlier. It is not caused by atherosclerosis and is unrelated to obesity, diabetes, or high cholesterol. A genetic predisposition exists, and smoking is the main modifiable risk factor.

faqs.question: How is fibromuscular dysplasia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: FMD is diagnosed by arterial imaging. CT angiography or MR angiography of the renal and cervical arteries typically shows the classic string-of-beads appearance of multifocal disease. Digital subtraction angiography remains the gold standard and allows simultaneous treatment. All diagnosed patients should have imaging of multiple vascular beds.

faqs.question: Is fibromuscular dysplasia treated with surgery?
faqs.answer: Most FMD cases are treated with percutaneous transluminal angioplasty — a catheter-based balloon dilation without stenting — which corrects the stenosis and improves blood pressure in 60-80% of patients. Surgery is reserved for complex cases. Cervicocephalic FMD is treated with antiplatelet drugs unless recurrent strokes require angioplasty.

faqs.question: Can fibromuscular dysplasia be cured?
faqs.answer: FMD cannot be permanently cured, but symptoms and complications can be effectively managed. Angioplasty normalizes blood pressure in 20-40% of patients with renal FMD. The arterial abnormality remains, requiring lifelong surveillance. Pulsatile tinnitus resolves in most patients after successful carotid angioplasty.

faqs.question: What is the difference between FMD and atherosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Atherosclerosis is plaque-based, affects older adults with high cholesterol and diabetes, and occurs in large arteries near bifurcations. FMD is non-plaque disease in young women affecting mid-vessel segments of medium arteries. Statins do not help FMD. FMD creates a beaded appearance; atherosclerosis creates irregular plaque narrowings.

faqs.question: Does fibromuscular dysplasia cause stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Carotid or vertebral artery FMD can cause ischemic stroke through arterial dissection or embolism from turbulent flow. FMD is one of the leading causes of stroke in adults under 50. Antiplatelet therapy reduces the risk of recurrent neurological events. Urgent imaging is needed whenever neurological symptoms develop.

faqs.question: What is pulsatile tinnitus and how does FMD cause it?
faqs.answer: Pulsatile tinnitus is a rhythmic whooshing or swooshing noise in one or both ears, synchronized with the heartbeat. FMD of the internal carotid artery causes turbulent flow near the petrous segment, which is transmitted to the inner ear. It is reported by 70% of cervicocephalic FMD patients and often resolves after successful angioplasty.

faqs.question: Is fibromuscular dysplasia hereditary?
faqs.answer: FMD has a genetic component. Family clustering is found in about 7-11% of cases, and variants in the PHACTR1 gene are associated with FMD, cervical dissection, and migraine. Inheritance is not straightforward Mendelian; multiple genes likely contribute. First-degree relatives with hypertension or TIA should be screened.

faqs.question: What happens if fibromuscular dysplasia is untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated renal FMD causes chronic hypertension with progressive hypertensive end-organ damage — left ventricular hypertrophy, renal impairment, and increased stroke risk. Untreated cervicocephalic FMD risks arterial dissection and ischemic stroke. Intracranial aneurysms left unmonitored carry a small but cumulative annual rupture risk.

faqs.question: Is FMD associated with aneurysms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intracranial aneurysms are found in 7-12% of FMD patients, most commonly in the circle of Willis. Renal artery aneurysms occur in 5-15%. All FMD patients should have intracranial and renal artery imaging at diagnosis. Annual surveillance MRA is recommended for known aneurysms until growth or size threshold triggers intervention.

faqs.question: Can FMD affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: FMD can complicate pregnancy through worsened renovascular hypertension, which requires careful antihypertensive management avoiding ACE inhibitors (contraindicated in pregnancy). Spontaneous arterial dissection risk may be elevated peripartum due to hemodynamic and hormonal changes. Women with known FMD should discuss pregnancy planning with their vascular team.

faqs.question: What is the string-of-beads appearance in FMD?
faqs.answer: The string-of-beads appearance is the characteristic angiographic finding of multifocal FMD — alternating segments of arterial narrowing and dilation along the mid-to-distal vessel, resembling beads on a string. It is caused by alternating fibromuscular ridges and pouches in the medial layer of the arterial wall and is essentially pathognomonic for multifocal FMD.

faqs.question: Should I stop smoking if I have FMD?
faqs.answer: Yes, absolutely. Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for FMD identified in large patient registries, present in 30-40% of affected patients. Continued smoking worsens arterial wall injury and is associated with faster disease progression and higher risk of dissection. Smoking cessation is a first-line recommendation for all FMD patients.

symptomKeywords: stroke young adult no risk factors
metaDescription: Fibromuscular dysplasia: non-atherosclerotic artery disease causing hypertension and stroke in young women. Imaging, angioplasty and prognosis reviewed.

sources.title: Olin JW, Gornik HL, Bacharach JM, et al. 2012. Fibromuscular Dysplasia: State of the Science and Critical Unanswered Questions. Circulation 125(25):3182-3199.
Gornik HL, Persu A, Adlam D, et al. 2019. First International Consensus on the Diagnosis and Management of Fibromuscular Dysplasia. Vasc Med 24(2):164-189.
Persu A, Touzé E, Meune C, et al. 2012. Diagnosis and management of fibromuscular dysplasia: an expert consensus of the European Fibromuscular Dysplasia Initiative. Eur J Clin Invest 42(3):338-347.
Debette S, Kamatani Y, Metso TM, et al. 2015. Common variation in PHACTR1 is associated with susceptibility to cervical artery dissection. Nature Genetics 47(1):78-83.
Kadian-Dodov D, Gornik HL, Gu X, et al. 2016. Dissection and Aneurysm in Patients With Fibromuscular Dysplasia: Findings From the US Registry for FMD. J Am Coll Cardiol 68(2):176-185.

Savard S, Steichen O, Azarine A, Azizi M, Jeunemaitre X, Plouin PF. 2012. Association between 2 angiographic subtypes of renal artery fibromuscular dysplasia and clinical characteristics. Circulation 126(25):3062-3069.
FMD Society of America. Patient Registry and Educational Resources.
